coredata.dc:title: Tuning the pore size of graphene quantum dots composite nanofiltration membranes by P-aminobenzoic acid for enhanced dye/salt separation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ya
Bi, Ran
Su, Yanlei
Li, Yafei
Yang, Chao
You, Xinda
Shen, Jianliang
Yuan, Jinqiu
Zhang, Runnan
Jiang, Zhongyi
coredata.dc:description: Nanofiltration (NF) is a promising technology for fast molecular and ionic separation. Pore engineering is a critical but challenging strategy to fabricate NF membranes with high permeability and precise sieving ability. Herein, we proposed a post-modification approach to regulate the pore size of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite NF membranes by using a small molecule p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) as a modifier. First, GQDs were used as the aqueous phase monomer of interfacial polymerization to react with trimesoyl chloride within the pores of the porous substrate, thereby fabricating large-pore-size GQDs composite NF membranes. After that, the self-polymerization of PABA within the pores of GQDs NF membranes would form nanoaggregates with different sizes, thus finely tuning the pore size of the as-prepared membrane. The PABA-modified membranes exhibited a range of pore sizes from 2.18 to 1.50 nm with the increased concentration of PABA. Specifically, the optimal PABA-modified membrane showed high water permeance of 128.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and complete interception of 1000 Da-higher dye molecules, with desirable dye/salt selectivity factors of 877.0 and 175.4 for Alcian Blue/Na2SO4 and Evans Blue/Na2SO4 separation systems, respectively. Moreover, the membranes exhibited outstanding physicochemical stability. This study provides a facile method for pore size regulation of NF membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration
Interfacial polymerization
Graphene quantum dots
P-aminobenzoic acid
Dye/salt separation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparing the NF-PABA-x membrane.
(a) ATR-FTIR spectra of the membranes; (b) XPS results of the membranes; (c, d) O1s high-resolution spectra of the PES substrate and the NF-PABA-1 membrane.

(a) Pore size distribution of the NF-PABA-1 and NF-PABA-6 membranes calculated by the N2 adsorption isotherm through the density functional theory (DFT). The insert is the pore size of the PABA-modified membranes; (b) MWCO tests of the PABA-modified membranes. The insert is the MWCO value of the PABA-modified membranes; (c) Particle size distribution of the self-polymerized PABA at different concentrations; (d) Illustration of the pore regulation by the PABA nanoaggregates in the GQDs composite NF membrane.

(a, b) Effect of the PABA concentration on water permeance, inorganic salts, and organic dyes separation performances; (c-f) UV–visible absorption spectra of the dye filtrate (black line), feed (red line), and concentrate (blue line) solution of the NF-PABA-3 membrane. The insert is the digital photos of the dye concentrate (left), feed (middle), and filtrate (right) solution.

(a) Dye/salt separation performances of the PABA-modified membranes; (b) Dye/salt selectivity factors in mixed and (c) single-solute separation systems of the NF-PABA-3 membrane and others reported membranes in literature (details are given in Table S3 and Table S4).

(a) Separation performances of the NF-PABA-3 membrane immersed in NaClO solution with different time; (b) Separation performances of the NF-PABA-3 membrane soaked in alkali solution; (c) Long-term operation stability of the NF-PABA-3 membrane under cross-flow filtration.
(a) Time-dependent water flux of the NF-PABA-3 membrane during antifouling tests of SA, HA and EB solutions; (b) The antifouling indexes of the NF-PABA-3 membrane.
Preparation conditions of the NF-PABA-x membranes.
The dye/salt separation performance of the NF-PABA-3 membrane.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Single salt (1000 ppm) or dye (100 ppm) solutions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mixed dye/salt (100 ppm dye and 1000 ppm salt) solutions.

head.ce:title: Tuning the pore size of graphene quantum dots composite nanofiltration membranes by P-aminobenzoic acid for enhanced dye/salt separation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofiltration (NF) is a promising technology for fast molecular and ionic separation. Pore engineering is a critical but challenging strategy to fabricate NF membranes with high permeability and precise sieving ability. Herein, we proposed a post-modification approach to regulate the pore size of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite NF membranes by using a small molecule p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) as a modifier. First, GQDs were used as the aqueous phase monomer of interfacial polymerization to react with trimesoyl chloride within the pores of the porous substrate, thereby fabricating large-pore-size GQDs composite NF membranes. After that, the self-polymerization of PABA within the pores of GQDs NF membranes would form nanoaggregates with different sizes, thus finely tuning the pore size of the as-prepared membrane. The PABA-modified membranes exhibited a range of pore sizes from 2.18 to 1.50 nm with the increased concentration of PABA. Specifically, the optimal PABA-modified membrane showed high water permeance of 128.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and complete interception of 1000 Da-higher dye molecules, with desirable dye/salt selectivity factors of 877.0 and 175.4 for Alcian Blue/Na2SO4 and Evans Blue/Na2SO4 separation systems, respectively. Moreover, the membranes exhibited outstanding physicochemical stability. This study provides a facile method for pore size regulation of NF membranes.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes are essential chemical raw materials widely applied in food, paper, textile, and cosmetic industries. Statistically, near 7 × 104 ~ 1.05 × 105 tons of dyes were discharged directly into water annually all over the world, bringing tremendous stress on the ecological environment [1]. The dyeing wastewater owning low biodegradability, complicated composition, and high chroma is a daunting water pollution source, and has gained extensive attention in the field of industrial wastewater treatment [2–5]. Especially, the high salts content (~6 wt% NaCl and ~5–6 wt% Na2SO4) from processes of dyes synthesis and application [6,7] inhibits the organic dyes biodegradation process, making it become the main obstacle for dyeing wastewater treatment [8]. Therefore, there is an urgent requirement to develop an efficient dye/salt separation technique to implement the advanced treatment of dyeing wastewater [9,10].

As an advanced pressure-driven separation technology, nanofiltration (NF) has the merits of low energy consumption, good selectivity and strong adaptability [11,12], and has been widely employed in several fields, including desalination [13–15], wastewater treatment [10,16–19], food [20,21], as well as pharmaceutical [22–24]. Meanwhile, NF shows a promising prospect for dyeing wastewater treatment as well. The molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of NF membranes ranging from 200 to 1000 Da [7,25] is consistent with the molecular weight range of most dyes. Polyamide (PA) NF membranes prepared by interfacial polymerization (IP) are the mainstream NF membranes. However, those traditional membranes typically show low permeability and separation selectivity due to their small pore sizes (<1 nm) [26]. Generally, the traditional PA membranes can intercept both dyes and inorganic salts (divalent salts: >90%, monovalent salts: >30–60%), while their selectivity for the two is unsatisfied, making it difficult to achieve efficient separation of dyes and salts. Preparation of NF membranes can retain dyes and allow salts to pass through has been the key to the application of NF membranes in dye desalination [26].

Pore engineering is a common approach to enhance the separation selectivity of NF membranes that reject undesired molecules by size exclusion dominantly. Over the past decades, numerous studies have devoted to altering membrane pore sizes [27–34], and two main strategies have been widely adopted. One approach is in-situ manipulation of the process parameters (e.g. diffusion rate [33,35,36], reaction rate [29], as well as reactive monomer category [28,31]) during the IP process to engineer nanopores towards a targeted size. The IP-based NF membranes exhibited excellent performance in ionic separation. The second approach is the post-modification of the original membrane pores formed by the IP to tune pore sizes, including solvent activation [37–39], functional group quenching [40], and cross-linking degree diminution [41]. The post-modification approach with simple operation and controllable condition was regarded as a prospective way to regulate the pore size of separation membranes. For NF membranes used for dye desalination, the adjustment trend of the pore engineering is to fabricate pores with the pore size between the dynamic diameters of salts and dyes. In order to obtain NF membranes with both high-permeability and high-selectivity, this principle undoubtedly puts forward higher requirements on the precise control of pore sizes and the uniformity of pore sizes. The development of a loose NF membrane with narrow pore size distribution and an adjustable pore size is considered to be the key to the NF membrane for dye/salt separation.

Herein, we combined the above two approaches to obtain the ideal NF membrane for dye desalination. First, we used graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the aqueous monomer to obtain a loose NF membrane [31], and then we proposed a post-modification method to regulate the pore size of GQDs composite NF membranes by using a small molecule of p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) as a modifier. Specifically, the PABA molecules with amine and carboxyl groups can easily anchor in the pores of GQDs composite NF membranes via the reaction between amine groups and the residual acyl chloride groups in the GQDs-TMC nanoaggregates [41]. And the in-pore self-polymerization of PABA would tune the pore size by forming different sizes of nanoaggregates. Ultimately, the chemical and physical properties of the membranes were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), field-emission scanning electronic microscopy (SEM), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). And the nanofiltration processes were carried out by employing organic dyes and inorganic salts to stimulate dyeing wastewater. This work provides a facile approach to tailoring the membrane pore size to break the permeability-selectivity trade-off.

Citric acid (CA), p-aminobenzoate (PABA), and 1,3,5-Benzene-tricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 99 wt%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). Poly(ether sulfone) (PES, Mw = 29000 g/mol) was obtained from BASF Co. (Germany). N, N-dimethyl-formamide (DMF), inorganic salts, organic dyes, and n-heptane were purchased from Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water in all experiments was produced by a lab reverse osmosis water purification system.

Firstly, the GQDs composite NF membranes were first fabricated via an in-situ IP [31]. More specifically, the PES ultrafiltration membranes were immersed into the GQDs solution with a concentration of 1 wt% for 15 min. After taking out and wiping the residual aqueous solution on the membrane surface, the membranes were soaked in the 0.1 wt% TMC organic solution for 2 min, followed by a thermally treated in an oven at 90 °C for another 15 min. Secondly, the nascent GQDs composite NF membranes were immersed into PABA aqueous solution with the concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.6 wt% for 3 min. Finally, the membranes were thermally treated at 60 ℃ for 10 min. The obtained PABA-modified NF membranes were stored in deionized water for 24 h before the subsequent characterization and performance evaluations. Briefly, the PABA-modified membranes were named as NF-PABA-x, where x indicates the relative content of PABA in the aqueous solution for the post-modification process. The preparation conditions of NF-PABA-x membranes were summarized in Table 1 .

The chemical structure of the NF-PABA-x membranes was analyzed by using ATR-FTIR (Nicolet 560, USA). The element components of the membranes were analyzed by XPS (K-Alpha+, UK). The surface and cross-section morphologies were investigated via a field-emission SEM (Nova Nanosem 430, USA). The root mean square roughness value (Rrms) of the membranes was characterized by AFM (Park XE-70, Korea) in the contact mode. The pore size of the NF-PABA-x membranes was measured by Micromeritics Tristar 3000 analyzer in the low-temperature nitrogen desorption method. The adsorption and desorption curves were analyzed by the density functional theory (DFT) method. The apparent water contact angle was measured by employing a contact angle goniometer (OCA15EC, Germany). The zeta potential of the membrane surfaces was characterized via a SurPASS Electrokinetic Analyzer (SurPASS, Austria).

The separation performances of the NF-PABA-x membranes were evaluated by a dead-end stirred cell filtration apparatus with an effective filtration area of 14.3 cm2. Membrane samples were loaded into a filtration cell (model 8050, Millipore Co., USA), and then the deionized water was loaded into the buffer tank. The water flux of the membranes tended to be stable after pre-pressing with deionized water at 2 bar for 30 min. Then the water permeance and separation performances were conducted at 0.5 bar. The water flux (Jw , L m−2 h−1) and permeance (Pw , L m−2 h−1 bar−1) were calculated as follows:
(1)
J w
=
V
A Δ T
(2)
P W
=
J w
Δ P
=
V
A Δ P Δ T
where V (L) corresponds to the volume of permeate solution in the experimental filtration time ΔT (h), A (m2) refers to the effective area of the membranes, and ΔP (bar) refers to the operating pressure.

Inorganic salt solutions (1000 ppm NaCl and Na2SO4) and dye solutions (100 ppm Orange GII, Methyl Blue, Evans Blue, and Alcian Blue) were used for the test of membranes separation performances. A certain amount of feed solution was added to the filter cell, and the tests were carried out at 0.5 bar. A conductivity meter (DDS–11A, China) and the UV–Visible spectrophotometer (EVOLUTION 220, USA) were used to obtain the concentrations of salt and dye solutions, respectively. The rejection rate (R, %) was calculated according to the following equation:
(3)
R =
C f
-
C p
C f
× 100 %
where Cp (ppm) and Cf (ppm) represent the concentration of the permeate and feed solutions, respectively.

For the dye/salt separation system, the separation factor (S) is a dominant performance criterion. S was defined as the ratio of salt penetration rate (Psalt ) to dye penetration rate (Pdye ) [42], calculated by the following formula:
(4)
S =
P
salt
P
dye
=
1 -
R
salt
1 -
R
dye

To explore the chemical stability of the NF-PABA-x membranes, the NF-PABA-3 membrane was immersed into 1000 ppm sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) solution for 30 days, we regularly tested its separation performance and replaced the solution every two days. Besides, we also recorded the separation performances of the NF-PABA-3 membranes twice a day when they were soaked in a harsh environment (NaOH, pH = 13). In order to ascertain the practical applicability of the PABA-modified membrane for dye/salt separation, the long-term operation stability of the NF-PABA-3 membrane was evaluated by a cross-flow device with an effective filtration area of 1.33 cm2 at the operating pressure of 1 bar. The experiment was continuously carried out for two weeks, including a week filtrating single Evans Blue solution (100 ppm) and another week feeding with Evans Blue (100 ppm) and NaCl (1000 ppm) mixture solution.

1000 ppm sodium alginate (SA), humic acid (HA) and 100 ppm Evans Blue (EB) solutions were employed as feed solutions to test the antifouling property. The antifouling indexes including total flux decline ratio (DRt , %), reversible flux decline ratio (DRr , %), irreversible flux decline ratio (DRir , %), and flux recovery ratio (FRR, %) were calculated to indicate the antifouling property of membranes. The antifouling parameters were calculated based on the following formulas:
(5)
D
R t
=
J
w 1
-
J F
J
w 1
× 100 %
(6)
DR
r
=
J
w 2
-
J F
J
w 1
× 100 %
(7)
DR
ir
=
J
w 1
-
J
w 2
J
w 1
× 100 %
(8)
FRR =
J
w 2
J
w 1
× 100 %
where Jw1 (L m−2 h−1 ), JF (L m−2 h−1 ), and Jw2 (L m−2 h−1 ) present the pure water flux, the flux for foulant solution, and the pure water flux of cleaned membrane, respectively.

The GQDs composite NF membranes were prepared by an in-situ IP between GQDs and TMC. Particularly, during the IP process, the steric hindrance effect of GQDs (Fig. S1a) impeded their diffusion rate into the organic phase, which led to forming rigid GQDs-TMC nanoaggregates in the nanopores of the ultrafiltration substrate [31]. PABA was used as a modifier to amend the nanochannels and voids among GQDs-TMC nanoaggregates. The fabrication process of the NF-PABA-x membrane was illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The chemical structures of the PABA-modified membranes were characterized by ATR-FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, compared with the PES substrate, the PABA-modified membranes exhibited a stretching vibration peak of C=O at 1725 cm−1 [31], which proved the ester reaction of the carboxyl groups at the edge of GQDs (Fig. S1b) with the acyl chloride in TMC during the in-situ IP. The peaks of the NF-PABA-1 and NF-PABA-6 membranes at 1635 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O of the amide groups [10] formed by reactions between PABA and GQDs-TMC nanoaggregates as well as the self-polymerization of PABA (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the appearance of N element in the XPS result (Table S1) approved the introduction of PABA. From the results of ATR-FTIR and XPS analysis, we demonstrated that the PABA modifier was successfully brought into the membranes.

Furthermore, as shown in Table S1, the XPS result of the NF-PABA-1 and NF-PABA-6 membranes possessed almost the same signal intensity of S element as the PES substrate. Our previous work has confirmed that due to the steric hindrance effect, the diffusion rate of nanoscale GQDs is lower than that of traditional amine monomers, which leads to the IP occurred in the pores of PES subatrate [31]. Therefore, PABA molecules can only be anchored in the membrane by reacting with the residual acyl chloride groups on the membrane pores, showing little influence on the element distribution of the membrane surface. We analyzed the O1s high-resolution spectra of the PES substrate and the NF-PABA-1 membrane, which were almost unchanged. From Fig. 2c, the O1s high-resolution spectra of the PES substrate were divided into two characteristic peaks. One peak at 532.3 eV corresponded to the oxygen in sulfonic groups (O=S=O) [28], and another peak at 533.4 eV symbolized ether units (C-O-C) [43]. The O1s high-resolution spectra of the NF-PABA-1 membrane emerged a new peak around 530.4 eV, which was derived from the chemical bonding (C=O) formed by several reactions (GQDs and TMC, PABA and GQDs-TMC nanoaggregates, self-polymerization of PABA molecules) [31,44]. The morphologies of the PES substrate and PABA-modified membranes were shown in Fig. S3. The PES substrate exhibited a smooth and porous surface. The PABA-modified membrane surfaces showed a non-porous structure, and no apparent selective layer was observed in the cross-sections. The composite NF membranes with loose structure have been proven to possess higher flux than that of the traditional PA NF membranes with a dense selected layer [31]. The surface roughness of the membranes was shown in Fig. S4. The roughness value almost could be negligible even if the PABA concentration reached up to 0.6 wt%. Specifically, the pristine GQDs composite NF membrane possessed a root-mean-square roughness of 16.1 nm, while the NF-PABA-6 exhibited the roughest surface with a value of 19.4 nm for Rrms. From the chemical analysis, SEM and AFM images, we verified the polymerization and post-modification occurred within the substrate pores [31].

The pore size distribution of the PABA-modified membranes based on Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) analysis [45] was shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S5. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of PABA-modified membranes (Fig. S6) indicated the existence of nanoporous volume in PABA-modified membranes. There were two types of pores in the PABA-modified membranes. The peaks around 1–3 nm corresponded to membrane surface with separable property, whereas the distribution near 45 nm corresponded to finger-like channels of the UF membrane formed by phase separation [46]. Specifically, there was a broad peak at 2.18 nm for the NF-PABA-1 membrane, in contrast with 1.50 nm for the NF-PABA-6 membrane. Other membranes exhibited a decreasing pore size ranging from 1.71 to 1.55 nm with the increase of the PABA concentration. Compared with the GQDs composite NF membranes, the PABA-modified membranes possessed more uniform pore sizes. That is because the PABA modifier with a small molecular volume distributed homogeneously in the pores, and reacted evenly with residual acyl chloride groups, thus generating more uniform pore sizes. We further investigated the MWCO of different membranes by employing dyes with varying molecular weight. In Fig. 3b, the MWCO of the NF-PABA-x membranes exhibited a descending tendency with the increasing PABA concentration, correlating with the changes of the pore sizes. Specifically, the NF-PABA-1 membrane showed an MWCO about 900 Da, while the NF-PABA-6 exhibited an MWCO about 500 Da. All the membranes showed larger MWCO than that of traditional PA NF membranes (300 Da), revealing their potential in enhancing dye/salt separation selectivity. To elucidate the pore-tuning mechanism of PABA molecules during the post-modification process, we proposed a structure model, as shown in Fig. 3d. The PABA molecule can be easily anchored in the pores of GQDs NF membranes via the reaction between amine groups and the residual acyl chloride groups. The self-polymerization of PABA within the pores of GQDs composite NF membranes would tune the pore size by forming the different sizes of nanoaggregates. In order to prove the self-polymerized characteristic of the PABA modifier, we obtained the particle size distribution of the PABA polymerized products under different initial concentrations by dynamic light scattering. As shown in Fig. 3c, the particle size of the poly-PABA gradually increased with the increment of the concentration. This was consistent with our assumption that the PABA modifier can effectively regulate pore sizes by forming nanoaggregates with different size within the pores.

The post-modification process narrowed the water channels and voids in the GQDs-TMC nanoaggregates, endowing the modified membranes with uniform pore sizes that contributed to the high permeance and excellent dyes rejection. As shown in Fig. 4 a, b, with the concentration of the PABA increasing, the PABA-modified membranes showed gradual reduction of water permeance (from 274.5 ± 8 to 83.7 ± 7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and enhancive rejection of dye molecules. When the PABA concentration increased to 0.3 wt%, the PABA-modified membrane could reject about 90% of organic dyes larger than 1 nm in size. Specifically, the NF-PABA-3 membrane exhibited nearly complete rejection of Evans Blue and Alcian Blue, dye rejection of 89.8% for Orange GII, 93.0% for Methyl Blue, compared with the GQDs composite NF membrane (64.5% for Orange GII, 76.0% for Methyl Blue). All the NF-PABA-x membranes rejected the dyes following the order of Alcian Blue (1298.86 Da) > Evans Blue (960.81 Da) > Methyl Blue (799.80 Da) > Orange GII (452.38 Da) (Table S2). Besides, we tested the UV spectrum absorbance of the dyes of filtrate solution, feed, and concentrate, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 c-f, the concentrate solutions presented a deeper color and higher absorption peaks than those of the feed solutions, both confirming the rejection of the dye molecules by the membrane. All the membranes displayed a low rejection rate of NaCl and Na2SO4, because the hydrated ionic radii (SO4 2− (0.38 nm) > Na+ (0.36 nm) > Cl− (0.33 nm)) are smaller than that of membranes pore (1.50–2.18 nm). The results approved that size exclusion was the eminent mechanism in the separation process of the NF-PABA-x membranes [12].

The PABA-modified membranes with excellent water permeance and high dyes retention, as well as low rejection of salts would be an ideal candidate for dye/salt separation. Therefore, we further investigated the dye desalination performance of the NF-PABA-3 membranes. For the dye/salt separation system, the selectivity factor (S) is a dominant evaluation index. We calculated the S parameters from Evans Blue and two inorganic salts (NaCl and Na2SO4) used in this work, and the results were presented in Fig. 5 a. The NF-PABA-3 membrane owned a relatively high S value as well as high permeance, thus we chose it as the sample for the subsequent tests. To estimate the practicability of the NF-PABA-3 membrane, the effects of inorganic salts on the separation properties of dye solutions were investigated [47]. As shown in Table 2 , after mixing the salts with Evans Blue, the NF-PABA-3 membrane showed a higher desalination rate, resulting in decreased dye/salt selectivity. This is probably ascribed to the boundary layer formed by the negative charged dye molecules on the negative membrane surface (Fig. S8b) would enhance the electrostatic repulsion interaction and hinder the transmembrane transport of SO4 2− and Cl− ions. While the enhanced electrostatic repulsion played delicate influence on the dye rejection. Compared with other reported works (Fig. 5b), the NF-PABA-3 membrane still showed great dye/salt separation performance potential in the dye/salt mixed system.

Fig. 5c summarized the dye/salt separation selectivity factors in this work and others reported membranes in literature (Table S4). The NF-PABA-3 membrane showed excellent dye/salt selectivity factor of 877.0 for the Alcian Blue/Na2SO4 separation system, which is an order of magnitude higher than that of the pristine GQDs composite NF membrane, and almost 3 ~ 10 times higher than the reported literature. The desirable water permeation and the excellent dye/salt separation property of the NF-PABA-3 membrane were contributed to the following reasons. On the one hand, in the in-situ IP process, the formed rigid GQDs-TNC nanoaggregates within the pores avoided excessive shrinkage of substrate bulk material during the thermal treatment that guaranteed high permeation. On the other hand, during the post-modification process, the fine-tuned pore size falling between the size of salt ions and dye molecules substantially elevated the dye/salt selectivity and maintained desirable permeance.

Chlorination degradation is an inevitable problem that limited the application of PA NF membranes in dyeing wastewater treatment. Dyeing industries commonly use NaClO to bleach cotton cloth, towels, and other fabrics, to achieve a preferable dyeing effect [48]. While the commercial PA NF membranes always show low tolerance to chlorine solution [49,50]. Recently, numerous studies have focused on alleviating the chlorination degradation of NF membranes by employing new IP monomers [31,51], embedding nanoparticles into membranes [52–54], or modifying membrane surfaces [41,55]. Despite significant advances, the mentioned strategies were faced with several bottlenecks, such as the complicated process of synthesizing new materials, the aggregation of nanoparticles, the compatibility of membrane materials with nanoparticles, and the increased transport resistance [12]. Herein, we investigated the separation performances of the NF-PABA-3 membrane in harsh environments. In Fig. 6 a, the pure water permeance of the membrane demonstrated little fluctuation during a chlorine exposure period for 720000 ppm·h, while the rejection ratio of Evans Blue remained over 96.2%. This is because the GQDs, as the primary material, were connected to TMC via ester bonds, which are more stable than amide bonds [56]. We further assessed the membrane performances in alkali treatment. As shown in Fig. 6b, the NF-PABA-3 membrane still exhibited high dye rejection (above 96.5%) after soaking in the alkali solution for 84 h. We also investigated the long-term operation stability of the NF-PABA-3 membrane by filtering Evans Blue solution (100 ppm) for a week, during which the membrane presented highly stable dye rejection (over 99.8%) and negligible permeance variation. Practically, the dyeing wastewater always contains high salinity, and the electrostatic interaction between ions and membrane surface would damage the membrane stability [26]. To better adapt to the actual separation system, we then tested the membrane stability by feeding with Evans Blue/NaCl mixed solution for another seven days. As shown in Fig. 6c, the NF-PABA-3 membrane still maintained stable dye rejection, salt retention and water permeance. Overall, the above results proved that the modified NF-PABA-x membranes possessed great potential applications in severe dyeing wastewater treatment.

Post-modification is a versatile approach to alleviate membrane fouling for reverse osmosis and nanofiltration membranes [57]. We carried out three kinds of foulants to assess the anti-fouling property of the membrane. The time-dependent normalized permeance was plotted in Fig. 7 a. Specifically, the antifouling indexes were shown in Fig. 7b. It is widely accepted that a higher FRR and a lower DRt indicate better antifouling properties. The NF-PABA-3 membrane exhibited excellent antifouling performance for EB solutions. During filtration of EB solution, the DRt value of the NF-PABA-3 membrane was 5.3% and the FRR value was 98.7%. The excellent antifouling performance was attributed to the fact that the hydrophilic NF-PABA-3 membrane (Fig. S8a) could form a hydration layer on the membrane surface by binding with water molecules, preventing the adsorption of organic molecules. Particularly, a strong electrostatic repulsive force between dyes and the membrane surface restrained the deposition of pollutants (Fig. S8b). Whereas, during the filtration of SA and HA solutions, the NF-PABA-3 membrane suffered relatively a higher flux decline ratio and lower flux recovery ratio. That’s because the natural organic matters (e.g. SA and HA) molecules with hydrophobic aliphatic and aromatic moieties were likely to become crosslinked and form a compact cake layer on the membrane surface [58], thus declining the permeance. The above suggested that both the surface properties and the foulant species contributed to the excellent antifouling property.

The PABA-modified membranes with high flux and precise sieving performance were prepared via in-situ IP followed by post-modification. The PABA modifier with a small molecular volume and homogeneous distribution reacted evenly with residual acyl chloride groups in the membrane pores, endowing the membranes with uniform pore sizes varying from 2.18 to 1.50 nm. The finely tuned pore size of the membranes fell between the size of salt ions and dye molecules, significantly elevating the dye/salt selectivity while maintaining the high permeance. Specifically, the optimal NF-PABA-3 membrane possessed permeance of 128.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and high dye/salt selectivity factors (Alcian Blue/Na2SO4: 877.0; Evans Blue/Na2SO4: 175.4), which were an order of magnitude higher than that of the pristine GQDs composite NF membrane. Moreover, the resultant NF-PABA-3 membranes exhibited excellent resistance to chlorine and alkali, as well as good long-term operation stability, holding great potential in dye wastewater treatment. Furthermore, this work provides a facile post-modification strategy in tailoring the membrane pores with rationally designed small molecules.

Ya Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation. Ran Bi: . Yanlei Su: . Yafei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Chao Yang: Methodology. Xinda You: . Jianliang Shen: . Jinqiu Yuan: . Runnan Zhang: . Zhongyi Jiang: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878217, 21961142013, 91934302), Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City (18JCZDJC36900), and Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (No. BP0618007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118372.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
